fact: August 17 is exactly 229 days into the year, which means there are only 136 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is typically prime time for meteor showers - the sky puts on its own fireworks show almost every clear night!
Cicadas are usually screaming their loudest right around this date because they're desperately trying to find mates before summer ends.
In late August, apples often taste extra crisp because the cool nights and warm days create the perfect sugar-to-acid balance in the fruit.
Spiders are typically at their absolute biggest and fattest right now because they've been eating bugs all summer long - some orb weavers are 10 times heavier than they were in spring!
The sun rises about 2 minutes later each day around August 17 in most places, so mornings get noticeably darker week by week.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, August 17 falls smack in the middle of winter, when some areas get their heaviest snowfall.
Monarch butterflies born around this time are super special - they're the generation that will fly thousands of miles to Mexico instead of dying after just a few weeks like their parents.
Tree leaves start their color-changing process right around now, even though you can't see it yet - the green chlorophyll begins breaking down invisibly.
August 17 falls during Leo season, when the constellation Leo the Lion sits highest in the night sky.
Grasshoppers and crickets typically reach peak loudness around this date because the males are singing love songs as fast as their legs can rub together.
Ocean water in many places reaches its warmest temperature of the entire year right around mid-to-late August, perfect for the best swimming.
